Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source entries: a .desktop file and a shell script `launch-app.sh`, both stored locally in the package directory, and two Debian packages (`.deb`) downloaded from the project's official GitHub releases URL using a version-pinned URL pattern. The `source` array uses no command substitution, `git clone`, or remote script downloads during sourcing. The `sha256sums` are explicitly defined and match the respective architectures, ensuring integrity.

Top-level variables and the `pkgver` are hard-coded or derived from static expressions—no dynamic fetching or execution occurs during sourcing (e.g., no `pkgver()` function that runs `git` or `curl`). The `package()` function contains installation logic that will not execute under `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. There are no obfuscated payloads, `eval`, or remote code executions in the sourced portion.

While the use of `.deb` archives might warrant scrutiny in a full audit, fetching them from the official release page of a trusted upstream during `makepkg` is standard practice for binary packages, and no malicious behavior is triggered merely by sourcing the PKGBUILD or downloading sources.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous execution during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous execution during source fetch and parse[/]

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, launch-app.sh...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for a binary package of a GitHub Desktop fork, sourcing `.deb` files from the project's official GitHub releases page. The download URLs are constructed predictably from the declared `pkgver` and point to signed, versioned releases at `github.com/pol-rivero/github-desktop-plus`, which is consistent with standard AUR packaging practices. The checksums are explicitly provided for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, and no checksum is skipped—this ensures integrity verification. The build process simply extracts the deb archive, relocates files to standard directories (`/opt`, `/usr/bin`, `/usr/share/applications`), and sets up a wrapper script and symlink, all within the package directory. No network operations, dynamic downloads, or code execution occurs during the build. The dependencies and optdepends are appropriate for an Electron-based desktop application. The `launch-app.sh` script is installed but not analyzed here; however, since it's part of the AUR source and not fetched at runtime, it remains within scope of standard review.

There is no use of `eval`, `curl|bash`, `git pull`, or other high-risk patterns. All operations are transparent and follow standard AUR packaging workflows. The package does not modify system files outside its scope or exfiltrate data. While the maintainer provides binaries instead of building from source, this is expected for a `-bin` package and not inherently unsafe.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified sources; no malicious behavior detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing launch-app.sh...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package with verified sources; no malicious behavior detected.
LLM auditresponse for launch-app.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `launch-app.sh` is a wrapper intended to read command-line flags from a user configuration file and pass them to the GitHub Desktop Plus application. It first checks for a primary config file (`github-desktop-plus-flags.conf`) and falls back to a secondary one (`electron-flags.conf`) if the first is absent. It reads the lines, skips empty ones and those starting with a `#` (comments), and accumulates valid lines into an array `flags`, which are then passed to the executable via `exec`.

All operations are confined to user configuration files under `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` or `~/.config`, which is a standard and safe location for user-specific configurations. The script uses safe array handling and proper quoting to avoid word splitting or injection. It does not perform any network operations, code evaluation (e.g. no `eval`, `base64`, `curl|bash`), or system modifications. The use of `set -euo pipefail` indicates defensive scripting practices.

The behavior is consistent with typical desktop application launchers that support custom command-line flags. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized file access. While loading flags from config files could theoretically be abused if the file is controlled by an attacker, that risk is inherent to the application design and not an instance of malicious injection in the AUR package.
</details>
<summary>
Safe launcher for user-configured app flags
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed launch-app.sh. Status: SAFE -- Safe launcher for user-configured app flags
Reviewed all required files.

Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing github-desktop-plus.desktop...
LLM auditresponse for github-desktop-plus.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `github-desktop-plus.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used by desktop environments to integrate applications into menus and launchers. It declares metadata such as the application name, description in multiple languages, icon, executable path, and supported MIME types for URI scheme handling (e.g., `x-github-client`). The `Exec` field correctly points to `/usr/bin/github-desktop-plus`, which is an expected location for a system-installed binary. All fields follow the desktop entry specification, and there are no signs of obfuscated commands, suspicious scripts, or network operations. The MIME types and keywords are relevant to the application’s purpose as a Git and GitHub client with multi-platform support. This file does not execute code directly nor perform file system operations — it is declarative and used solely for desktop environment integration.

No malicious behavior is present, such as embedded shell commands, data exfiltration, or unexpected system modifications. The use of multiple language-specific comments is normal for internationalization. The file conforms to standard packaging practices and poses no security risk on its own.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed github-desktop-plus.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
Reviewed all additional files.

<details>
The script is a standard launcher that reads optional user configuration flags from `~/.config/github-desktop-plus-flags.conf` (or a fallback `electron-flags.conf`) and passes them to the main binary at `/opt/github-desktop-plus/github-desktop-plus`. The only file access is reading user-owned config files, which is normal and expected for a launcher script. There is no evidence of supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
</details>

<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard AUR binary package that downloads a pre-built `.deb` file from the project's official GitHub releases (using a pinned version tag `v${pkgver}`) and extracts it for installation. All source files have valid SHA-256 checksums. The `package()` function performs only expected operations: extracting the archive, moving files to `/opt/` and `/usr/bin/`, installing a custom desktop file, and creating a symlink for the CLI tool. There are no obfuscated commands, no unexpected network requests, no exfiltration of data, and no execution of untrusted code outside the upstream release. The `launch-app.sh` is a plain script shipped as part of the package source, intended to launch the application. This file is consistent with normal packaging practices and poses no supply-chain risk.
</details>
